design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate castle chambers, defeat enemies with whip and subweapons, reach and defeat boss, progress to next stage
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (hearts for subweapons, limited health) vs. enemy relentlessness; forward momentum vs. platforming precision
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Classical monster hunter (Belmont) methodically clearing Dracula's castle using whip mastery and item strategy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unforgiving knockback damage mechanic (Simon gets launched into the air on hit) creates emergent speedrunning tech and demands mastery of enemy manipulation. No difficulty mode in original version—single intended challenge. Whip-centric combat with subweapons as strategic decision points rather than defaults

difficulty.lives_system: 3 lives per credit; continues restart at stage beginning
difficulty.continues_system: Game Over returns to stage select; progress lost
difficulty.checkpointing: None within stages; progress saved only via FDS save feature (not in cartridge versions)
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Low in NES cartridge (Famicom re-release adds Easy Mode option). Damage knockback physics can be abused by skilled players for sequence breaks, but this is emergent exploit rather than designed accessibility

progression.upgrade_system: Subweapons (axe, holy water, rosary, cross) with limited hearts (mana). Whip upgrades (leather→chain) via candle drops. Temporary power-ups (invisibility, time-stop watch) from candle destruction
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stages unlocked sequentially after boss defeat. Stage select available after game over
replayability_hooks: Speedrunning via enemy manipulation and damage knockback abuse
Challenge of beating game without taking damage (subweapon hearts remain unused)
Alternate subweapon routing (choose strategically based on stage)
FDS save feature enables progressive challenge runs

world_building_techniques: Title screen framing as horror film (animated bat, castle motif)
Stage progression through recognizable Gothic locations (graveyard, tower, forest, bridge)
Enemy thematic relevance (mummies in tombs, medusa heads in caverns, fishmen in water)
Ending cutscene confirming Dracula's defeat and Simon's victory

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-memorization encounters testing subweapon selection and attack timing. Each boss occupies specific arena space; defeat triggers stage progression
notable_bosses: Mummies (Tombs) - dual synchronized enemies, limited attack patterns
Medusa (stage mid-point) - projectile enemy requiring dodging
Frankenstein's Monster (stage late-game) - high HP, slow but powerful
Dracula (final) - multi-phase battle with health threshold triggers

faction_indicators: Simon Belmont as established monster hunter (series protagonist)
Dracula as archetypal evil seeking resurrection (implicit cycle threat)
Holy items (rosary, cross, Bible) signal Belmont's clerical/blessed warrior role
danger_signals: Animated bat on title screen as territorial threat
Progressively difficult stages signal escalating danger toward castle core
Boss health meters visible (01A9 RAM address) create tension through attrition
Enemy knockback on damage creates feeling of being overwhelmed if careless
environmental_storytelling: Stage 1-3 graveyard setting establishes undead/mummy enemies
Stage 4-6 tower/forest transition from outdoor approach to interior castle
Stage 17 clock tower boss arena with gear backdrop (Frankenstein's Monster domain)
Final stage 18 confrontation in Dracula's chamber

sources_used: tasvideos - Enemy behavior documentation, movement mechanics, damage knockback physics, RAM addresses
tcrf - Unused mechanics (cross/Bible items, subweapon variants), version differences (FDS save feature vs cartridge), regional differences (Easy Mode in Famicom re-release)
